ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken curry' class (FOODON_00002490); no Maharashtra-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked, with sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Maharashtra-specific chicken curry entry. A00LC (cooked chicken with sauce/gravy) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Maharashtra Chicken Curry or a sufficiently close prepared-dish equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Maharashtra Chicken Curry is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a rich spiced gravy, usually accompanied by rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, Indian style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for Indian-style chicken curry. Maharashtra-specific curry uses goda masala and coconut, which slightly elevates fat and energy compared to a generic North Indian chicken curry. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken curry entry (per 100 g edible portion) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken curry, home-prepared' (FDC ID 174591) to confirm plausibility. The ICMR-NIN source is the primary authority for this Indian dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maharashtra Chicken Curry. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous Indian chicken curry dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Indian chicken curry is a protein- and fat-dominant dish with low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100 g), placing it firmly in the low-GI category. GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 14.4 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 5.0. Carbohydrate per serving: 4.8 g/100 g × 300 g = 14.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Maharashtra Chicken Curry provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Maharashtra Chicken Curry has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of about 5 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) and sodium (~380 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Maharashtra Chicken Curry provides beneficial lean protein and anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and ginger, its saturated fat and sodium content at restaurant serving sizes warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, particularly those following a low-sodium or low-saturated-fat dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maharashtra Chicken Curry is a traditional spiced chicken dish originating from the Maharashtra state of western India. It is distinguished from North Indian chicken curries by the use of goda masala, a fragrant spice blend unique to Maharashtrian cuisine that typically includes dried coconut, sesame seeds, stone flower (dagad phool), bay leaves, cloves, cinnamon, and black pepper. The curry is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic in oil, adding tomatoes and the goda masala paste, then simmering bone-in or boneless chicken pieces until tender in a rich, moderately spiced gravy. Freshly grated or desiccated coconut is often incorporated, lending a slightly sweet, nutty depth to the sauce. The dish has a deep reddish-brown colour, a robust and complex spice profile, and a medium-to-high heat level from dried red chillies. Texturally, the chicken is tender and the gravy is thick and coating. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich dish (~13.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~9.2 g/100 g), low carbohydrate (~4.8 g/100 g), and a low glycemic index (~35), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. The sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) is clinically relevant for hypertension management at restaurant serving sizes. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is served in Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms, typically accompanied by steamed rice, chapati, or paratha.
common_user_queries: How is Maharashtrian chicken curry different from North Indian chicken curry?
Is Maharashtra Chicken Curry good for diabetics?
How many calories in Maharashtra Chicken Curry?
What are the main spices in Maharashtrian chicken curry?
How much protein is in Maharashtra Chicken Curry?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Indian chicken curry data (the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central. No exact Maharashtra-specific entry exists in any approved nutrient database; the adaptation accounts for coconut and goda masala additions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Indian chicken curry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (Indian style) and cross-checked with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174591. No exact Maharashtra Chicken Curry entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on Indian chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021 AJCN); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g reflects trace levels from cooking oil; this is a plausible low value consistent with the source. 6. Halal status is flagged as true given the dish is chicken-based and typically prepared in halal-certified Indian restaurants in Singapore; this should be verified at the restaurant level. 7. The field survey source (input) is used only for food identity and restaurant context, not for nutritional values, in compliance with the forbidden-source policy.
